item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Zyn, do you just lurk on the Recent Posts page

Yes. εїз

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Zyn, do you just lurk on the Recent Posts page and leap in with relevant links/info when you see somebody asking for help, like a superhero with extremely specific priorities?)

Creidieki, one issue is that a lot of Foundation lore is ambiguous, and is interpreted different ways by different authors. "Does the black moon howl?" has been used as a motto, a passcode prompt, a key concept in an SCP-001 entry, and many more besides. The only real common thread is that it's spooky and mysterious, and somehow associated with the Foundation. Having a clear reference for this sort of thing can be a bit tricky!

I tried to write this article such that even if you're not super familiar with Foundation lore, you can pick up enough from context clues to get the gist. The Ethics Committee is exactly what it says on the tin, for example. or is it?probablyhmmmdon't worry about it.

Anyway, the Foundation can be pretty lore-dense sometimes, but I hope you still enjoyed the article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, thanks. It would still be helpful if references like this were linked, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The search bar is your friend! http://www.scpwiki.com/forum/t-5468586/does-the-black-moon-howl

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the Black Moon thing from something in particular? I keep seeing people using it, but no one seems to link it to anything. It's frustrating when authors seem to assume a particular reading order for the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Black Moon geas gives me Laundry Files vibes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I echo most of the above praise, but what I appreciated most was that you actually depicted the Foundation as an organisation – a bureaucratic machine of interlocking committees and departments, with factional politics and, as your own brilliant innovation, a fleshed-out separation of powers, which reflects the Foundation as an organisation of state-like scale and complexity.

Depicting the cogs and blueprint of an organisation is such a hard kind of "world-building" to do, and creative people are rarely good at it; those who are are typically smart-but-boring corporate apparatchiks with job titles like "operations consultant". Which is a shame, because the workings of large organisations determine so much of happens in the real world, and I wish humanity was better at telling compelling, digestible stories about them.

But here, we see something like *The Wire: Secure, Contain, Protect*, which absolutely rules, and I'd love to see more of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good stuff - a clever idea, good dialogue (maybe a tad clunky in places, but not enough to really bother me), and a solid unfolding of the plot. As others have said, the use of the Black Moon was inspired, and it ends up being a really nice bit of worldbuilding. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Foundation drug wars are always a terrifying concept, even if the occasional portal to where math is not correct is a rare occurence.
Thanks for reading, friends!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's slightly ironic that you wrote "keeping SCP-yaddayadda from tearing open a hole in the space time continuoum" given that this was exactly what happened here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am extremely impressed.

You implement some very high-concept, deep worldbuilding elements into this article with a seamless narrative flow that is VERY tricky to pull off. The exposition and explanation of key concepts, like the use of the Black Moon, is woven very naturally into the Ethics Committee layout context - you're never too overt about it, but not too vague either.

You've also written some very strong dialogue here. The characterization is clear, if a bit surface-level in terms of Lee Corbett and their perceived malice, but it functions well.

My only criticism is the final encounter with 05-10 slightly overstays it's welcome. The final explanation of events was immensely satisfying, folding relatable human fallacy with fascinating intra-universal concepts like mnestics and the noosphere, but it comes quite early. From there, the verbal fencing match just caries on and on, reiterating concepts and throwing heaps of new terminology in that, while engaging, don't add much to the narrative; nowhere moreso than the deep dive into the Council's social architecture. It's a distraction from a fantastic story, leaving the conversation feeling too heavy and protracted for an "incidental conversation".

Regardless, I loved this. Fantastic read, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was absolutely fantastic! I don't have much to add that hasn't already been more eloquently worded than I would in the comments above, but I wanted to come out and personally say how much I enjoyed it too. Clever and incredibly well-paced, +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for representations of the SCPverse that are very out of the ordinary "canon." Black Moon as a geas is brilliant, I love it. Though it's not the focus of the story being told, the idea of a drug so anomalously unstable that it allows someone's nightmare to punch a hole through reality is also fantastic. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Superb.

The characterization of o5s, the implications of the ethics committee and the marked difference in tone and in how they do things compared to the regular foundation structure. Ive seen comments that dislike the casual banter between e5 and o5, I must say it made the article for me.

It isn't that casual, Mallory is playing with fire while walking a tightrope and both know it. It gives it this wonderful sense of dread, wondering if and when Ten is going to yank the leash or deem her a liability. Which is then made explicit further down the post.

All in all I can't find which part of this I love most.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much for the feedback! I definitely intend to write more in this style, with these characters. I was originally thinking that this would tie into some tales, but now I’m considering having a series of interconnected scps, any of which could individually stand on their own, but each of which explore some new aspect of EC/O5 interactions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always keep coming back to this one as an unforgettable article. This SCP really provides a taste of an exploration into Foundation internal politics which I dearly adore, and manages to integrate non-anomalous, somewhat mundane idiosyncrasies (i.e. conversational interviews, voice recording devices) and anomalous qualities (i.e. the whole Black Moon situation). It's something which I deeply want to see again, be it from earlier articles or later ones.

Either way, great work with this. Upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is some excellent storytelling. You both embraced and enhanced the in-universe organizational structure masterfully. The Foundation was a character here. Plus, I always love a battle of grey morals. Brought me out of my lurkin' cave to vote and comment. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is absolutely fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept of poetic analysis being used with a geas is incredibly entertaining to me. Good stuff :-) +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your feedback! One thing I'd like to mention is that the idea of work/life balance in research like this is very much taken from real-world situations. Even in top-secret specialized areas of study at the Foundation, the right answer is always going to be "hire more people." Saying that the Foundation can't find people because they don't have recruiting drives is a bit of a copout. There's no reason that recruiting people through front organizations or poaching them from government labs doing similar research should be hard, just expensive.

I wholeheartedly think that the O5's were absolutely wrong. Find room in the budget to be nice to your employees, it's not that hard. More often than not in real life, the impediment is ego and powertripping directors who don't want to ask for more money, or harsh senior management who would retaliate for people requesting more of a budget for personnel. 99.99% of the time, the conditions that lead to these abusive working environments are due to human issues, not any sort of immutable fact. I think the Foundation is a more interesting place to write about if it reflects the sort of problems that happen in reality, as well as universe-ending threats and horror.

(Also, sorry if it was unclear, but everything that E5-2 says in her office is E5-sealed. The jab she made about body cams was to say that even if they left her office, it would still be sealed to that clearance level, so there was no reason for O5-10 to try).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a bit torn on this article. I love parts of it, yet other parts just don't hit me quite right.

On one hand, there are aspects about it which sort of rub me the wrong way - the mere fact that we have a lady who is apparently drinking buddies with a freaking 05 just screams 'mary sue' - and yes, I know she's really not, and in fact is actually pretty well rounded and clever, and its obvious the article has a lot of time put into it - yadda yadda yadda. My point is, in my opinion, it's kinda, well, cringy, having a casual conversation with the higher ups of the foundation that's apparently not even under clearance.

On the other hand, though, I really really really like that part of the article. It actually, genuinely humanizes both the 05 and the ethics comittee, a hell of a lot more than most articles do, where they're pretty much only mentioned as a way to say 'oh this next part isn't meant to be read by the actual foundation but because you're an omni-god writer you can look at it XD". You actually make them feel like people, and while it is a little cringy how this random ethics committee lady we've never heard of is apparently powerful enough to mock the 05s, it's still a very interesting take on it, and one that I can appreciate.

+ show block
– hide block

No, what I'm more opposed to is the resolution to this. So, the foundation now has better work hours and ethics. Great! Fantastic! Good for them, especially the whole drug-report thing. The thing is, though, while its great they now have fifty-hour-work weeks instead of hundred-and-twenty-hour ungodly weeks, at the same time… did it ever occur to ethics lady that maybe the foundation needs these ungodly shift hours? I mean ffs, half the skips these days are constantly on the verge of erasing reality or whatever. Maybe it's more important for us to save the entire fucking universe, than it is for researchers to be able to sleep an extra four hours a day.

I know, this sounds cold, and to an extent it is - but honestly? I kind of agree with the 05's methods on this one. Yes, she was basically drugging her employees to get them to constantly work, but at the same time, that sort of insane work hours may just be the only thing keeping SCP-yaddayadda from tearing open a hole in the space time continuoum. SCP foundation isn't exactly open with fucking recruiting drives, and I'd assume it takes years to train people up to levels like this. In other words, the station may well have only like five people maintaining the fucking universe's cohesion, and now we want to give them goddamn vacation days while the thermodynamic reactor goes critical or whatever.

TL;DR - I genuinely hate to no vote, as I prefer to treat skips as "they impressed me, or they didn't work", but in this case, I just can't go either way. It's so well written and I love many parts of it, especially the personalities established, but at the same time the concept is so flawed and silly that I can't, in my opinion, give it an upvote.

TL;DR-TL;DR: I think the 05 was right.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me log in to comment about how much I like it. I adore how you've used the Black Moon here—is that original?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Black Moon geas (and associated concept of poetic analysis) and the implied existence of an E5 Council are now headcanon. This is a damn good skip.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: My next scp will just be told through the titles of nested tabs
This is good, but it could use more tabs.
Hot damn that was fast! Guess y'all like Ethics Committee content huh :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your feedback! And thank you for catching the missing redaction as well!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although the Foundation in this article is quite different from the Foundation in my headcanon, this is a very good read. We see in everyday life these regular, ethical and somewhat expected regulations - such as working hours limit or policies beneficial to employees like in this case. However sometimes we take those for granted so much that we don't always know or pay attention into why and how things come to work that way. And sometimes it's better not to be involved in. I find that to be really interesting.

Love the characterization, dialogues and the narrative behind.

Also, there's an "Elliot" not redacted in the final SCP document.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great formatting and the amount of effort put into this article is commendable, but more importantly, the story is interesting.

Clear +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback! I based "Wnoroski" off a friend-of-a-friend of mine, but digging further, it looks like that's one of those things where American immigration officials horribly misspelled a European name about a hundred years ago. It's the same reason why there are people in America with the last name "Jager," "Jaeger," "Yager," "Yeager," and "Yaeger." You're absolutely right, it's definitely not the original spelling!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great article. Some really nice worldbuilding. I always appreciate when the O5's are given a bit of personality instead of being the Scary People Up High. Also, this is a really small pet peeve, but Wnoroski isn't really a real surname, a much more accurate pick would be Wronowski. Again, just nitpicking but as a Polish person it really didn't look right to me.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your feedback! I definitely intend to keep going with these characters, and write more SCPs and tales about the Ethics Committee. This is my second E.C. SCP, but it definitely won't be my last! (Also, Mallory is a woman, fwiw).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The global changes are also a problem in and of themselves. The logistics, bureaucracy, time commitment, budget, and many other implementation elements would make changes like this nigh impossible. This isn't even taking into account the fact that different cultures across countries deal with work and professionalism differently. All these changes happening in go because of one anomaly localized in one country is difficult to believe, especially when it's presented so plainly.

There are several things I would like to talk about, but due to time constraints I'll have to address only this for now.

The thing is that companies, by design, create their own micro cultures separate from the outside world in order to achieve better results. Take for instance the gaming industry1 doing what the Foundation is doing here.

Hierarchy is also absolutely fundamental in this scenario. Moreover, unlike other real-world organizations, the Foundation's purpose requires to be extremely flexible and efficient at making organization-wide changes to a weekly basis, most likely. It wouldn't be surprising that the employee's handbook has a mandatory rule saying "if the Abyss stares back, address it with a thumbs up (left hand) for no less that 3 seconds".

Footnotes
1. the most know instance of a real-world industry culture that crunches the hell out of their blue-collar employees.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is some good world-developement and exceptionally good character development. I love Mallory and you just made me want an entire series of him and his wacky ethic-adventure. I am deadpan serious about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoting without hesitation for reclaiming the black moon for something other than edgy passwords. The worldbuilding is excellent, and while the overtly mythological winks about O5-10 are not a thing I'm a fan of, the rest of the article is too good to dislike.

I do have to echo ARD's thoughts on the final revision, though. Tugs at my suspension of disbelief a little too hard.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That makes sense. It's hard to remember just how varied everybody's canons can be, especially when I tend to think in one that is not quite as popular an interpretation. I think I will leave this piece as-is, as it's been up long enough for a good number of people to have voted on the current iteration, but I will definitely keep that in mind for future articles!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your feedback! In-universe, the fine details of the revised drug policy are in the employee handbook and HR policies, but I figured putting some of it in the containment procedures would help the reader understand more about what was being changed. In retrospect, it would have made sense to limit that, and make the loophole a bit more plausible. I'm glad you enjoyed other parts of the SCP though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like a lot of what comes up to the ending, but I’m not a fan of the ending itself. Even in-universe, it’s weird that the Foundation’s revised drug abuse policy is effectively being conveyed as containment procedures for this SCP. I don’t find E5-2’s trickery to be a convincing argument for this method of conveyance either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That makes sense, and since "there is no canon" it's a perfectly fine interpretation. In this case what would improve the article would be to make it clear that this is the scope of the Foundation in this context. Since the general interpretation is more or the less the Foundation being pretty big world-wide, letting the reader know that for this story one should view the Foundation in this manner would let them adapt their suspension of disbelief. I think by the time the reader gets to the 05 conversation they should know this as to make the exchange sink better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God damn, this was a good read. It was riveting the entire time, with no unnecessary fat and just the good stuff.

And I love Mallory not only being a damn good detective, but how she managed to wrangle bureaucracy into getting something substantial done. The dialogue and characters were compelling, and it's also a very important look into how overwork literally kills people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, sure sure. In my head this was localized more or less to Site-19. I tend to think of the Foundation more as a smaller organization with lots of power behind it, based out of a few permanent sites in America and one or two in Europe, while sending operatives to other parts of the world. I realize that's not necessarily the most common interpretation, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh that I understand, and I like the idea of showing the EC as both competent and important in the Foundation, but it just doesn't feel possible for changes that big to happen world-wide. For example, if I were in charge of a site in South America and I'm told to do massive changes to the structure of work hours, day offs, security, audit schedules, disciplinary action, and all other policies because of something that happened in Europe, I'd die just by trying to work out the logistics. There would be no way I could restructure the way the whole place works because of that, and this would extend to any other country.

I just can't really picture the entire Foundation being so homogenized throughout the world that massive changes like this can be done worldwide and this easily. Different branches of the Foundation would have their own highers up to deal with, and that would include their own Ethics Committee. Having to change basic structural policies because one higher up from a different continent said so? Can't picture that.

This would work a lot better if the scope was brought down a lot. Instead of world-wide changes, make it so that it's in that region, and make the changes flexible enough that other places can actually reform without having to cease all operations for God knows how long until they are implemented.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an article with strong direction and equally matched execution. Much of my enjoyment or non-enjoyment of the piece is not based on the polish of the narrative but my personal appreciation for the direction the author is taking it in. I find it to be a refreshing take that intersects bureaucracy, the limitations of authority and the responsibility to make moral decisions within them.

I'm not sure how I feel about the direct mechanics of the piece- the Black Moon invocation is at some points a deus ex machina sort of tool but at the same time I don't see an easy way for the piece to explore what it's trying to without a similar sort of shortcut.

Overall I'm upvoting because I think this piece genuinely contributes to exploring a nuanced view on the Foundation as an organizational structure in a manner that I haven't seen before, and doing it with a strong voice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback! The idea I was going for here was that E5-2 was using the fact that the ethics committee can only officially edit Special Containment Procedures as a backdoor way to sneak in a comprehensive overhaul of the way the Foundation handles drugs and addiction management. It's really not about this particular SCP, but about the Ethics Committee using a loophole to make some sweeping changes to the Foundation at large. I was implying that by this exchange:

O5-10: I'll put in a good word. Regardless, I'm not sure what you intend to do with the inquest from here. The Ethics Committee only has authority over Special Containment Procedures and research protocols, not discipline. I'm honestly surprised that whatever leaps of logic you're taking are sound enough to keep the Mandate in effect.

E5-2: Go back and re-read the first Ethics Committee Review doc. 'Special Containment Procedures must describe whatever measures are necessary to prevent further instances of SCP-5236 from manifesting in the future.'

O5-10: Oh. Oh. Well played by you as well, Mallory.

This wasn't really a story about any particular SCP, but rather about the EC taking an opportunity to actually do something quite substantial, while using the extremely narrow (though also, extremely high-ranking) powers available to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Ethics Committee evolving from a joke into an actual serious and important component of the Foundation is one of the best changes the site has gone through the years, which is why I'm always up for an article that showcases their working procedures. However the way E2 and the structure of the Foundation are portrayed really kills it for me.

The fact that E2 can make global changes to Foundation procedures and is pals enough with 05s to know their personalities and get cheeky with them in personal conversations makes me think that 52 is really high up in the Committee's hierarchy. Considering the sheer size of the Foundation and how much they deal with in a daily basis, I can't picture someone that high up doing so much footwork for a single SCP, when logistically she would be mostly delegating and overseeing the big picture. Even for something with this big an implication it would make more sense for it to start with an EC grunt and the problem be subsequently sent up the hierarchy until it actually reaches one of the big bosses, because I really can't imagine people with this much power and responsibilities having enough time to deal with individual cases.

The global changes are also a problem in and of themselves. The logistics, bureaucracy, time commitment, budget, and many other implementation elements would make changes like this nigh impossible. This isn't even taking into account the fact that different cultures across countries deal with work and professionalism differently. All these changes happening in go because of one anomaly localized in one country is difficult to believe, especially when it's presented so plainly.

I feel like this type of narrative needs to be much longer in order to be properly paced and to make the escalation in scope make sense, as well as to give proper details and context as to how a seemingly normal EC problem leads to a Foundation-wide conspiracy.

I know this is basically mostly headcanon I'm basing my criticism off, but the story going from 0-100 in the course of one conversation and the fact that the Foundation is so sloppy at hiding their inner covert operations makes it hard to believe they can get anything done without being discovered by an employee with some basic sleuthing skills.

There's a bunch of details that I like, and the concept of itself is compelling, but as it is I can't suspend my disbelief enough to enjoy the story. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the perfect gift for the headcanon that has everything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is some superb dialog right there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this a lot. You made something very mundane into an extremely palpable, entertaining, and hard hitting story. The dialogue was good and the analysis logs were a nice touch. I liked the ending as well, it was nice to see the Foundation looking out for their employees, you don’t see that too often in articles

Great job!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was thinking the same thing. It never occurred to me that it could have some kind of anomalous significance as a phrase, and it's a really enriching detail to include. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, this is good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, good catch! That's a mistake on my part, I've fixed it. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly? I like it when someone manages to make the world a better place. I also like the explicit use of the Black Moon phrase as a geas. There's lots of good bits of little worldbuilding in here, and the dialogue is pretty fun to read.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A really great article. Good use of taking a slightly boring concept and extrapolating from that to make something engaging. +1

Minor thing:

E5-2: Who would I talk to if I wanted to find out who makes it?
J.T. Wnoroski: Look, this isn't… I mean, I don't want… I don't know. I don't know.
E5-2: Does the black moon howl?
J.T. Wnoroski: The winds crushed our throat.

This doesn't actually match the interview transcript. Not sure if this is intentional meddling by E5-2 or if it's an out-of-universe mistake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback! For the ending, I kind of wanted to write a scp where the story actually does turn out okay, more or less. Evil has been defeated by… well, something a bit less Evil, hopefully. Though Sasha is still dead, and now E5-2 owes a Favor to O5-10. I see your point, though, it could have more of a punch if I went at it from a different angle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thoroughly enjoyed this. The world-building is as palpable as it is fascinating — although the final iteration is a tad unsatisfying. Seems everything turned out alright in the end, for the most part.

Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The overall concept of this article is pretty nice and I enjoyed the way you formatted everything.
+1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to The Great Hippo and SoullessSingularity for crit in IRC, and to rubberchickenzilla in the forums!

For more of my stuff, check out my Author Page! For another Ethics Committee SCP with a similar format, check out SCP-4452.
